epigraph.text: I am the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end.
epigraph.quote: The integral of an analytic function around a closed curve is zero—the most important theorem in analysis.

content.text:
We now prove the foundational result of complex analysis. Cauchy's 
original proof assumed continuity of $f'$; Goursat later removed this 
assumption, showing that mere existence of $f'$ suffices.

content.title: Cauchy-Goursat Theorem (Triangle Version)
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on a domain $D$, and let $T$ be a triangle 
(including interior) contained in $D$. Then
\[
 \oint_{\partial T} f(z)\,dz = 0,
\]
where $\partial T$ denotes the boundary of $T$ traversed counterclockwise.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Subdivide $T$ into four congruent triangles $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ by 
connecting the midpoints of the sides.

center
tikzpicture[scale=1.5]
 % Main triangle
 (A) at (0,0);
 (B) at (2,0);
 (C) at (1,1.5);
 (A) – (B) – (C) – cycle;
 
 % Midpoints
 (AB) at (1,0);
 (BC) at (1.5,0.75);
 (CA) at (0.5,0.75);
 
 % Subdivisions
 (AB) – (BC) – (CA) – cycle;
 
 at (0.6,0.35) { $T_1$};
 at (1.4,0.35) { $T_2$};
 at (1,1) { $T_3$};
 at (1,0.55) { $T_4$};
tikzpicture
center

The integrals along the interior edges cancel (each traversed twice in 
opposite directions), so
\[
 \oint_{\partial T} f = \sum_{j=1}^{4} \oint_{\partial T_j} f.
\]
By the triangle inequality, 
$\left|\oint_{\partial T} f\right| \leq \sum_{j=1}^{4} \left|\oint_{\partial T_j} f\right|$, 
so at least one subtriangle $T^{(1)}$ satisfies
\[
 \left|\oint_{\partial T^{(1)}} f\right| \geq \frac{1}{4}\left|\oint_{\partial T} f\right|.
\]

Repeat: subdivide $T^{(1)}$ and choose $T^{(2)}$, etc. We obtain a 
nested sequence $T \supset T^{(1)} \supset T^{(2)} \supset \cdots$ with
\[
 \left|\oint_{\partial T^{(n)}} f\right| \geq \frac{1}{4^n}\left|\oint_{\partial T} f\right|.
\]

The triangles shrink to a point $z^* \in \bigcap T^{(n)}$. Near $z^*$, 
since $f$ is differentiable:
\[
 f(z) = f(z^*) + f'(z^*)(z - z^*) + \epsilon(z)(z - z^*)
\]
where $\epsilon(z) \to 0$ as $z \to z^*$.

The integral of $f(z^*) + f'(z^*)(z - z^*)$ (a linear function) around 
any triangle is zero. Thus
\[
 \oint_{\partial T^{(n)}} f = \oint_{\partial T^{(n)}} \epsilon(z)(z - z^*)\,dz.
\]

If $L^{(n)}$ is the perimeter of $T^{(n)}$ and $d^{(n)}$ its diameter, 
then $L^{(n)} = L/2^n$ and $d^{(n)} = d/2^n$. On $\partial T^{(n)}$, 
$|z - z^*| \leq d^{(n)}$ and $|\epsilon(z)| \leq \epsilon_n \to 0$. By ML:
\[
 \left|\oint_{\partial T^{(n)}} f\right| \leq \epsilon_n \cdot d^{(n)} \cdot L^{(n)} 
 = \epsilon_n \cdot \frac{dL}{4^n}.
\]

Combining: $\dfrac{1}{4^n}\left|\oint_{\partial T} f\right| \leq \epsilon_n \dfrac{dL}{4^n}$, 
so $\left|\oint_{\partial T} f\right| \leq \epsilon_n \cdot dL \to 0$.

Thus $\oint_{\partial T} f = 0$.

content.title: Cauchy-Goursat Theorem (General Version)
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on a domain $D$, and let $\gamma$ be a closed 
contour such that $\gamma$ and its interior are contained in $D$. Then
\[
 \oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 0.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The proof triangulates the region bounded by $\gamma$ and applies the 
triangle version to each piece.

marginNotes.text:
Behold, I am the one who has laid as a 
foundation in Zion a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone.

marginNotes.author: Augustin-Louis Cauchy (1825)
marginNotes.text:
This fundamental proposition... 
whose consequences are so extensive.
